The **districts of England** (officially, **local authority districts**, abbreviated **LADs**) are a level of [subnational division of England](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_England "Subdivisions of England") used for the purposes of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England").[[1]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-1) As the structure of local government in England is not uniform, there are currently four principal types of district-level subdivision. There are a total of 296 districts made up of 36 [metropolitan boroughs](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"), 32 [London boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs"), 164 two-tier [non-metropolitan districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") and 62 [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England"), as well as the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") and the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly") which are also districts but do not correspond to any of these other categories. Some districts are styled as [cities](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough") or [royal boroughs](/wiki/Royal_borough "Royal borough"); these are purely honorific titles and do not alter the status of the district or the powers of their councils. All boroughs and cities (and a few districts) are led by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayors_in_England "Mayors in England") who in most cases is a ceremonial figure elected by the district council, but—after local government reform—is occasionally a [directly elected mayor](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayors_in_England_and_Wales "Directly elected mayors in England and Wales") who makes most of the policy decisions instead of the council.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Districts_of_England&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Subdivisions of England](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_England "Subdivisions of England")

Before the establishment of districts in the 1890s, the basic unit of local government in England was the parish, overseen by the [parish church](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") [vestry committee](/wiki/Vestry_committee "Vestry committee"). Vestries dealt with the administration of both [parochial](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/parochial "wikt:parochial") and [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") governmental matters. Parishes were the successors of the manorial system and historically had been grouped into [hundreds](/wiki/Hundred_(county_subdivision) "Hundred (county subdivision)"), which had exercised some supervising administrative function. However, these powers ebbed away as more and more civic and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial "Judicial") powers were centred on [county towns](/wiki/County_towns "County towns").[[2]](#cite_note-2) From 1834 these parishes were grouped into [Poor Law Unions](/wiki/Poor_Law_Union "Poor Law Union"), creating areas for administration of the [Poor Law](/wiki/English_Poor_Laws "English Poor Laws"). These areas were later used for [census registration](/wiki/Registration_district "Registration district") and as the basis for [sanitary provision](/wiki/Sanitary_district "Sanitary district").[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 1894, based on these earlier subdivisions, the [Local Government Act 1894](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1894 "Local Government Act 1894") created [urban districts](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") and [rural districts](/wiki/Rural_district "Rural district") as sub-divisions of [administrative counties](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county"), which had been created in 1889. At the same time, parish-level local government administration was transferred to [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish"). Another reform in 1900 created 28 [metropolitan boroughs](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough") as sub-divisions of the [County of London](/wiki/County_of_London "County of London").

The setting-down of the current structure of districts in England began in 1965, when Greater London and its 32 [London boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") were created. They are the oldest type of district still in use. In 1974, metropolitan counties and non-metropolitan counties (also known as "[shire counties](/wiki/Shire_counties "Shire counties")") were created across the rest of England and were split into [metropolitan districts](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough") and [non-metropolitan districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district").

The status of the London boroughs and metropolitan districts changed in 1986, when they absorbed the functions and some of the powers of the metropolitan county councils and the [Greater London Council](/wiki/Greater_London_Council "Greater London Council"), which were abolished. Since 2000, powers are again shared (on a different basis) with the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority").

During the 1990s a further kind of district was created, the [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England"), which combined the functions and status of county and district.

## Metropolitan boroughs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Districts_of_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Metropolitan boroughs")]

Main article: [Metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough")

Metropolitan boroughs are a subdivision of a [metropolitan county](/wiki/Metropolitan_county "Metropolitan county"). These are similar to unitary authorities, as the metropolitan county councils were abolished in 1986. Most of the powers of the county councils were devolved to the districts but some services are run by joint boards and organisations. The districts typically have populations of 174,000 to 1.1 million.

## Non-metropolitan districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Districts_of_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Non-metropolitan districts")]

Main article: [Non-metropolitan district](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district")

Non-metropolitan districts are second-tier authorities, which share power with [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council"). They are subdivisions of [shire counties](/wiki/Shire_county "Shire county") and the most common type of district. These districts typically have populations of 25,000 to 200,000.

In this two-tier system, county councils are responsible for some local services, such as education, social services, and roads, while district councils run other services, such as waste collection, local planning, and [council housing](/wiki/Council_house "Council house").

The number of two-tier non-metropolitan districts (also known as **shire districts**) has varied over time. Initially, there were 296; after the creation of single-tier unitary authorities in the 1990s, 2000s, 2010s and 2020s, their numbers were reduced to 164 by 2023.

### Unitary authority areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Districts_of_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Unitary authority areas")]

Main article: [Unitary authorities of England](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England")

These single-tier non-metropolitan districts are responsible for running all local services in their areas, combining county and district functions. They were first created in the mid-1990s, and often cover large towns and cities as this is deemed more efficient than a two-tier structure. In addition, some of the smaller counties such as [Rutland](/wiki/Rutland "Rutland"), [Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire") and the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") are unitary authorities. There are a total of 62 unitary authorities, the latest ones introduced in 2023.

Unitary authority areas are a type of non-metropolitan district; most are established as individual counties containing a single district, with a district council but no county council. [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"), [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") and [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire") were established as counties with a single district, but have non-metropolitan county councils with no district council. In practice, these function in the same way as other unitary authorities. [Berkshire](/wiki/Berkshire "Berkshire") is unusual, being the only non-metropolitan county, with no county council, that has more than one unitary authority district within it, each of which is not a county.

## London boroughs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Districts_of_England&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: London boroughs")]

Main article: [London boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs")

The 32 London boroughs are sub-divisions of [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London"). They were established in 1965. Between 1965 and 1986 a two-tier structure of government existed in Greater London and the boroughs shared power with the [Greater London Council](/wiki/Greater_London_Council "Greater London Council") (GLC). When the GLC was abolished in 1986 they gained similar status to the unitary authorities. In 2000 the [Greater London Authority](/wiki/Greater_London_Authority "Greater London Authority") was established and a two-tier structure was restored, albeit with a change to the balance of powers and responsibilities.

Each London borough is responsible for many of the services within their area, [such as schools, waste management, planning applications, social services, libraries and others](/wiki/London_boroughs#London_borough_councils "London boroughs").
